reviews.review_text:
Outward is a very unusual RPG game with heavy focus on survival and lots of walking. The learning curve is brutal and since there is no saving mechanism, player has to live with the consequences of their failures. But once you learn how to craft OP weapons and utilize dodge / parry / stagger / traps / ranged attacks / buffs, the combat becomes fair, almost too easy.

This game is my GOTY. Not many games can capture that wander-lust feeling like this one. Everything you see in the game is traversable and almost everywhere you go is some hidden loot in a dungeon.

Outward is a game by Guillaume Boucher-Vidal made for Guillaume Boucher-Vidal. That's it! That's the filter through which you need to fit in order to enjoy this game. If you don't then you will bounce off this game hard, but if you do then you will find a special gem tailored to a certain type of gamer. 
I picked the definitive edition at 2$ (lol) after some nut-job reviewer I follow recommended it and I got waaaaay more entertainment out of it than I expected... because I am the type of gamer (or close) that this was made for. 
I like wandering and exploration - and those are the things that you will do in Outward almost all of the time. I like walking by some weird ruins and exploring a dungeon that they hide; I like finding some super heavy armor that I may or may not use and then slowly and painfully dragging it back to town to sell for way less than I had hoped for; I like gathering random ingredients and crafting foods and potions that a gobble up like a maniac before a difficult fight covering the left side of my screen in over 20 buff icons; I like finding some unfinished artifact that I can later upgrade in a certain spot using certain conditions. 
THIS IS ALL COOL TO ME and, obviously, to the game author itself. To some others as well I guess, since there will be an Outward 2 it seems.

With that out of the way - if you step back and examine this thing objectively from what it offers ... it sucks... BAD! The mono-maniacal vision of how the game is supposed to be played trumps everything else; common sense first and foremost:
- No, there is no sense to why the quests are so bare-bones and the npc's so cardboard. This is what the author wanted
- No, there is no reason why half the story is related in out-of-game posts on the dev website. This is what the author wanted.
- No, there is no sense as to why most of the mechanics in-game are not explained and it's just a bunch of icons that you have to guess. This is what the author wanted.
- No, there is no reason to have so many timed quests. This is what the author wanted.
- No, there is no reason why you have only 8 hotkeys and NO AUTO-RUN OPTION. This is what the author wanted!!!!
There are reviews here made by people who call this stuff "realism" or whatever but I would not take those seriously since the definitive edition patched out a bunch of the "realism" already in order to make the game waste slightly less of your time and feel less like a chore. For example having separate stashes in each city is... not nice (this was one of the most downloaded mods before it became part of the main game).
So do yourself a favor and use the wiki as well as modding the hell out of this if you are bouncing off of it since it's a single player game where the vision and the term "quality of life" live on separate continents. I used the Thunderstore launcher after finishing the main quest and added hud and multi-hotbars but there are a TON of others which fix almost everything about the game that is grating.
I'll probably play a few more times through this exploring different skill tree combinations (only 3 of those per character as well but this limitation is more understandable) and different faction quests since there is a very cool legacy item upgrade system. 
I'll do this instead of trying Outward 2 when it comes out because I have the feeling that the author's vision has not changed and it will just be the same sandpaper experience at launch.
P.S: I do not recommend "The Three Brothers" DLC as it's obviously unfinished and adds "grinding" for resources to this game because of course that's what was missing. On top of that all of the enemies were tuned with inflated stats and resistance ignore (???) so that you don't have too much fun with your late-game build. Very bad addition to the game with it's one interesting idea of city building made as annoying as possible

One of the few games that turns you from a hobo to a hero. A fantasy game with a realistic survival mechanic. You're gonna get butt hurt by everything till you learn that running away is the best strategy. If you've always wondered how you would fare in an Isekai where you never get OP  then this is the game for you.

The concept of the game is top-tier. The idea of an action RPG with intense survival mechanics and realistic progression deeply resonates with me. However, a game that prides itself on difficulty MUST ensure that the combat is extremely refined which is simply not the case here. Enemies frequently move in ways that defies physics, and hit detection is bizarrely inconsistent.

This game isn't good but if you research enough stuff to the point where you know absolutely everything about the game, you will be able to have fun. It is an absolute pain to begin the game and most content and systems are loosely integrated if that. The combat and movement is clunky but if you have nothing better to do, you will get used to it. The Outward 2 Beta is out now and it is absolutely broken in every way Outward 1 is so... watch out folks.

This is a game designed for a specific type of player. If you can appreciate a true challenge, hard save settings, hard survival settings then this is for you. It is a rare gem of a game that rewards the player for meticulous planning and clever thinking. Combat is great and meaningful, gameplay is superb. 
I shall give one example: 
I was scouting south/east of starting city at night, darkness total, music ambient, I decided to turn my lantern on as I could see the outline of a cave opening.... I was attacked by 4 hyenas from behind. killed one, knocked out by the others.... I thought that was it... no it wasn't. a loading screen came up and the writing was something like this (from memory) "You awake to the noise of flesh and bone being torn apart and devoured, you look right and see a half eaten leg beside you, you realise the hyenas have saved you for a later dinner...." I got up, realised only one hyena had stayed behind to feast picked up a shiny iron sword from the poor chap being eaten (now have first iron sword) absolutely battered that mother****** went outside and another 5 hyenas were just off to the right, so I snuck left and made my slow bloody, stamina depleted crawl back to my home Village THIS WAS AN EPIC EXPERIANCE!

I am right at start of the game, basic weapons, leather armour, primitive backpack, no magic. Honestly I am a hardcore RPG single player gamer and this is a masterpiece, a welcome experience when most games hold your hand and make the whole journey about following markers and instant fixes.

Looking forward to seeing the rest of the developers hard work.

Roll on the rest of this epic journey.
Ngl, it's pretty niche on if you'll enjoy it but totally worth trying cuz once it clicks it's so satisfying

Let me preface this review with the disclaimer that I historically DO NOT like survival games where you have to monitor hunger and thirst and other statuses. Most of the time these types of games leave me feeling bored, afraid to explore much for fear of starving/dying of thirst, and directionless after a few minutes. 

HOWEVER, I played Outward with my boyfriend for a few hours tonight, and something about it convinced me to play over the two hour maximum before refunding. I think the story/quests help to give the game a direction and encouraged me to work towards certain goals that other survival games don't really give you. 

So, after 3 hours of playing, I can safely say that I will likely play this game more with my partner in future play sessions. Good job, devs!

What I was hoping for was a fairly generic, open-world single player sword-and-sorcery adventure-ish simulator; which this game sort-of-is, but the entire experience has been dreadfully underwhelming and rather disappointing. 

- The combat is rather unsatisfying, 
- The lore seems to be trying too hard to be different
- The introductory story feels contrived and simply isn't compelling 
- The environmental visuals are okay, but dated
- The environmental lighting is pretty useless, particularly in the dark
- There is no jump. Wut?

I have died way too many times in this game. Honestly, it is way more fun if you bring a friend along to suffer with you. There is a bit of a learning curve, but exploring the world becomes an absolute blast once you figure things out. The game just throws you in and lets you forge your own path with zero hand-holding—you really feel like an ordinary nobody who is completely out of their depth. Half my playtime is spent crawling back to the nearest town at a snail's pace because my inventory is packed with junk I refuse to leave behind, simply because I desperately want the cash. It is an awesome game overall, and I definitely recommend checking it out.

Store page for this game is extremely confusing as someone that owns the og game. def edition is on sale yet if i purchase that i dont get a copy of it(according to steam) so it says i have to buy the 10 dollar dlc then i can get a copy of definitive. seems extremely roundabout especially considering you cant see that dlc in your library but all dlc are still searchable on steam and my copy in the library says definitive in the title card but not anywhere in game. just seems slimy

The AI in this game is ♥♥♥♥. The RNG is ♥♥♥♥. The combat is ♥♥♥♥. This game is way over rated. Play it, find out for yourself. No cloud saves. Be aware when uninstalling temporarily or when switching PC's. You will lose absolutely everything.

Do not buy this game, 
if you want to progress with another player. Its not possible to have the same progress at the same time. Only the host will have the important progress rewards, like joining a faction or skills. If you want to enjoy a coop game find a good one and not this crap. Its not worth a dollar!

the minute to minute gameplay is fun but honestly this game is flooded with bad choices i just cant understand.

some quests are timed and will have nearly gameplay ruining consequences if you fail them and the game does a bad job of letting you know nearly every quest is timed.

if you end up buying the dlc youll have to start a whole new save file to play it, you cant use an existing save to play the new content like literally every other game out there, that was an instant refund.

getting stronger is incredibly limited, you have these things called "breakthrough" points and you only have 3 a playthrough which means youll have to plan out your build you cant refund them or gain more.

gaining mana costs you health and stamina in pretty large amounts and you cant refund this its a permanent change, this however is one of the few things the game actually tells you.

this game is incredibly unforgiving to newer players with its poorly explained permanent choices and timed quests, because of that i cant reccomend it to new players
The worst combat I've ever experienced in a game. If they fixed that, this game would be great.

This is tough love. It is a good and rewarding adventure, but you may drop it in the first area, when a pearlbird beaks the ♥♥♥♥ out of you. The learning curve is steep, and you can't do overleveling since there are no levels. There is gear, knowledge, wits and experience. Learn the rules, exploit weaknesses, gather resources and use them wisely, and the most important: learn when to run. There is no XP, so unless there is no specific item that the enemy drops, or there is no quest or trigger on it's head, you don't have to fight them.
It is hard, punishing, sometimes not quite fair, and not in the way a soulslikes do. Always drink clear water. tea is your best friend, food and patience above healing potions and get the fiery poker.
This game is horrible, the combat is clunky, the graphics are horrible and did i mention combat sucks?

Terrible controls, especially on joypad ,feels like a bad joke. Janky poorly made combat in general. Constant issues with co-op for unclear reason. The idea may be great but it feels like devs ignore the good things established by other games just "to be original". Kinda inventing badly designed bicycle.
I am sure this is a pretty good game for the unemployed kids, but this game just doesnt respect your time

Outward is definitely not a game for everyone. Some people just want a power fantasy out of their RPGS,  or hate games with learning curves. Some of the people in the reviews here just suck at video games. 

But Outward is quite honestly one of my top 5 rpgs of all time. This isn't my main account, but i've dumped hundreds of hours over multiple playthroughs. You start as an indebted serf in a world so dangerous most people never leave their home city, and are forced to adventure in the greater world to avoid losing your house. You'll manage heat, cold, hunger, thirst, and exhaustion, on top of a slew of other statuses and diseases you can contract ( all of which you can apply to others). There is no permadeath, which is good, because survival is brutal. There's dozens of different weapons and eleven different "classes", of which you can train three max at a time, which makes your 'build'. Do you want to place magical and mechanical traps and lure in foes with a bow? You can do that. Do you want to apply enchantments to your giant sword that make it swing incredibly fast and blend foes? You can do that. Martial artist spearman who uses extreme speed to dodge and summons ghosts to distract people? Sure. Gunmage? Yeah. Paladin who summons his sword? Whatever you want. It's extremely flexible and provides huge variation as you go through each storyline( as you can only go through one each playthrough). 

Which, by the way, the story is actually quite good. Although there's not really any cutscenes and dialogue is relatively short, you get a really vivid picture of a living, breathing world, with fascinating lore that isn't trying to be like a tolkien clone, if you know what i mean. The factions are all nuanced, and balanced, and besides the Scourge there isn't really any clear villains. 

Combat is tight, stamina management is important, and you will be panicked during every fight. You have a huge amount of flexibility in how you engage and how you fight, and you'll need it. Even a lowly ostrich can run up and whoop your ass if you're caught tired or wounded. Every fight needs to be planned out, twice as much if you use traps, magic, or ranged weapons. But done properly, you can become extremely powerful. I've never played a game that rewarded the struggle towards leveling as much as this one did.

What can I say? Pick it up on a sale if you're still doubting, and playing it before Outward 2, which will be released soon. Get two copies and play it with a friend- it's twice as good. But pick it up, because you're missing out on one of the best rpgs you'll ever play.

It's always fun to see negative reviews from folks who literally couldn't have even gotten out of Chersonese  with the amount of playtime that they have. For reference, while I only have 80ish hours on Steam, I have around 100 on Xbox (I've actually beaten the main quest with a friend on there) and have played this game on and off basically since it launched in 2019 and throughout the pandemic onwards. I'm playing again now, and felt like writing a review since I've seen the game's score fluctuate for the past year or so on here.

Oh, Outward. Where to begin with a game that attempts to defy every expectation and deny every comfort of the titans of the genres it was built on top of? I think for one, describing what the game isn't doing might help. This is not a Soulsborne-like/lite, nor does it give the player the absolute freedom to do whatever they basically feel like doing, reminiscent of games like Breath of the Wild, Craftopia, or Palworld. That would imply that there's some level of help or even proper tutorial that the game has in store for you in order to get the player eased into the mechanics of the world. No, Outward has nothing of the sort. Instead, this game is rough around the edges, deeply unbalanced at times, and graciously unsparing when it comes to bad luck, clunky movement and combat, and environmental hazards. These aren't necessary failings, however (with the exception of the combat, because sometimes it does feel insane to fight enemies by just bum-rushing them with light attacks until they fall), since many of the negatives are either balanced out by sticking with the game and learning its rhythm with time or by brute forcing your way through Aurai itself with cheese and luck. 

I instead believe that Outward was moreso guilty of the sin of not being extremely explicit in its marketing when people looked at it as a unique experience for the first time in 2019, and now again with this remastered version. RPG, Open World, ARPG, and Difficult are all tags that do get at parts of the experience of playing Outward but never properly get there all the way. The one thing that makes Outward so very different than other games in its genre (and also plays to its strength the best, imo) is the survival RPG aspect. Living through harsh enemy/environmental encounters, dealing with the movesets of new mobs you haven't seen before, and finishing the faction questlines that take several days off of your lifespan are all aspects of the game that balanced out by the positive feelings you have when everything (and I do mean EVERYTHING) begins to lean your way. Getting new skills/weapons, buying armor/robe upgrades, travelling to new locations, getting quest rewards, finishing enchantments on gear, and eventually staring your own colony feel like major accomplishments in the same way that extracting in a game like Tarkov or Marathon feel like a victory over the forces of nature. In Outward's case, it's more literal; you survived another day, and are all the stronger for it. 

Nothing on the market for as cheap as this game is now (and might be forever, since the 2nd game is coming out very soon) will scratch that itch like Outward does, to me at least. Are there aspects that I mod out now and then, or change in order to fit my liking? Yes, absolutely. As others have mentioned, some aspects of Outward are purely slow and monotonous for the sake for simulating the slower aspects of real life (which I don't think I need to say, doesn't play well with a ton of people since that's what video games are meant to be an escape from). But, keeping the majority of the grind and difficulty that comes from having to carry 60lbs with a 50lb capacity bag and slow walk to town, or attempting to beat back a bandit camp with no allies or help but a bunch of elemental rags and potions are essential to the enjoyment of the game. 

In other words, unlike a game like Elden Ring, where the struggle feels unrealistic and fantastical but is deeply challenging and satisfying on a mechanical, technical, and even narrative level, Outward is the same  type of game for survival realism (or as realistic as a ARPG can be), tone, and the overall experience of (and I know people find this word pretentious nowadays and for good reason) immersion in its world. And with mods, the game's like a 8.5/10. I rest my case.
Outward 2 hype. i haven't gotten into this game yet, but i keep coming back. great combat and world building, good starting story, seems cool. beginning is a bit tedious.

I like challenging games and Outward is definitely one of them. But that's not the only reason I enjoy it. The game is incredibly immersive and I've spent countless hours playing it with my wife, exploring the world and fighting (or sometimes running from) monsters. If you enjoy challenging survival games with a strong sense of adventure and exploration, you'll probably love this one.
It is a really fun adventure game. No map indicated guide, no hand holding, just you and a will to go outward to wherever you want and can.
very engaging game many skills , many types of magic , runes , various weaops , crafting , alchemy, cooking like d everything
Lost all my gear in my friends game, played the game and got all of it back and join with them again. We got captured and jumped down the hole to not be in jail. Fun game
Definitely not for everyone but it is an amazing game if you love challenges.

It doesn't hold your hand. There's a learning curve. Once you get going you won't want to stop. It's a large game with several large maps, loads of dungeons and wide open spaces filled with monsters. Survival fantasy at it's best!

I played this when it originally came out, but had a little trouble getting super into it. Now that it has been a few years since the definitive edition I can say that it has held up well and is a great adventure game. It plays stronger as a co-op game, but it really does have a sense of genuine adventure that is seldom seen in most games. 

If you give it an honest chance and maybe watch a video or two on it you'll come to see that it's a pretty unique game with a lot of fun to be had. There's also tons of ways to build your character and all of your strength is gained through ability trainers and gear. No leveling, just an honest adventure.

I bought this one on sale, I wouldn't bought it for the full price, Ever. 

This game feels so dated and I still gave it a chance, But it threw me off so bad. 

Sorry. Unless you're longing back to the early 2000's, I wouldn't recommend.
if you can't manage to dodge and block and treat it like a souls-like game with stat managment for hunger, thirst, sleep and such. it's not really for you. but it's a fun game.

Grab it while its five bucks - it has a lot of charm. Hardcore charm. Played the Outward 2 Beta and wanted to support the devs - neat game they crafted, cant wait for the Second Entry !

----

After 10 hours I gotta tell you this is THE adventure simulator for me, finally managed to tackle a few dungeons, find a leyline and unlock magic and this was a personal quest I chose for myself none the game gave me and it felt incredibly rewarding to be finally able t ocast spells. 10/10 would torture myself again with an overburdened backpack.

I really want to like this game, but there are two main issues for me:
1) janky camera makes me want to vomit! Causes severe motion sickness, so i just can't play this without having to lie down after a few minutes of game play
2) I really like to save scum. That's just my style of playing, wanting to be a perfectionist. But alas, if you die, you fail the timed quest. 

If it wasn't for the camera/motion sickness, i would have stayed longer and gave it a longer try. Unfortunately this game just wasn't meant to be.
This game uses mouse acceleration and you just cant deactivate it. This, for me, is a game breaker. Not recommended.
Great game open exploration for you (very hard) or with a friend (somewhat easier...), plays like a dark souls but still very unique, Elden Ring could learn from this game. lacks PVP though

I recommend this game, mainly for it's exploration. I like not having fast travel, but there should be a mount system to unlock or progress. Don't die repeatedly to a strong enemy. You just have to take it slow and reset burned stats for each encounter or else you'll get frustrated; this isn't dark souls. To get started, use ranged weapons and equipment like traps to your advantage. It's genuinely fun to have threats that just need to be avoided while exploring. There aren't many open world games where you just walk like this and have survival mechanics. The idea of drinking tea for medicinal purposes and to keep you warm in the snow is just great.

The game is ok, it's not bad, but it is far from amazing.
Pros:
-Game is stable and runs smoothly
-Dynamic Events
-Good Lore (Requires some looking around or using the wiki)
-A good balance of challenging but fair
-Great looking armor and weapons
-Decent graphics

Cons:
-World feels empty
-Lack of accessibility options
-Lack of any option of any form of guidance (No map marker, no quest marker, just thrown to the wolves)
-Very primitive AI/CPU of enemies and allies
-Lack of any real story (You won't even realize you beat the game)
-No true online multiplayer (unacceptable when the ability to do so is there)

Overall, the game is fun for what it is. It is here to relieve you of that Dark Souls itch from time to time without spending the money on Dark Souls. The world in this game is a big size, but it feels so empty. You will be running around A LOT without any sense of direction other than using landmarks and basic map reading skills as there is no assistance to anything. The game has a bit of different enemy variety, however the cpu of the enemies in the game are is VERY primitive. The cpu in this game only has two ways they will engage you, either use a spell or range attack from a distance and continue to do so even if there is something in their way blocking their attack, or they will run up to you with no care in the world to be demolished. The game does have local multiplayer as well as with friends, but there should have been a way to join randoms in their adventures if the player allowed it, would have made more replayable that way. The game is fun for what it is and there are plenty of secrets to explore. At the end of the day though, it just feels empty and leaves you wanting more that you will never get. You will never know when you beat the game, the game tells you nothing, it is all up to you to explore that. I wish there was a in-between rating, because the game is ok. What made me give it a good score is that it does draw you into exploring, and that does get twice as fun when you do it with a friend. It reminds me so much like Fable and I love Fable, and because of that this review is a little bias. Get the game on sale and it won't disappoint, but it is not worth full price.

EDIT: After fully beating the game, I realize that the game make things way over-complicated for no reason what so ever. I started the Three Brother DLC and placed a building that I now do not want. I am permanently locked out of placing an enchanting building, permanently locking me out of armor enchantments, for NO reason. This is VERY bad game design. The devs should be ashamed of themselve. At least by doing this, I can finally just drop the game and do something else in my spare time. I may try out Outward 2: Pirate Edition when it comes out to see if it has similar stupid mechanics in the game like this one does before I spend money on it.

Honestly, I hate this game. So buy it and lets hate it together. 

There are so many aspects of it I wish were different. 
The Devs are SO full of themselves. "Have you ever fought a monster in real life?" what a load of -
There are like TWO voice actors for the whole game!!
The game rotation takes the walking from charming to fetch quest levels of fun REAL QUICK.
Idk why anyone plays this game at all... its just...

The concept is SOOOO GOOD. They hit the nail right on the head with the world building, the MAGIC oh man the magic, and the skills. UGH!!

I can't wait for the 2nd one to come out so I can hate that one to. 

I bought this game like thee times (at least once for me and then a couple for friends, and I think I had to upgrade to the definitive edition at some point) and I STILL got more than 1hr/$1 of fun out of it.

10/10 would recommend...

Rise and shine trog spawn! 
Love this game especially with a friend! It really captures that feel of going on an camping adventure! You aren't special you have no skills and have to work for everything. This game can be extremely easy if you work inside its constraints or very difficult if you don't. 6/10 solo 9/10 with a good friend!

This is a genuine "Love it or hate it" game. Open World survival with Soulslike combat and survivability. 

You will die. A lot. 
You will restart. A lot.
Will you keep playing a lot?

I did.
mods make it fun. vanilla is good but with some QOL mods like meditation and more spells make it exceptional.

This game is really a breath of fresh air for me. I had a lot of fun with it. Does this game cater to a particular type of player? Yes. It caters to the type of survival RPG player who is content with very little handholding.

It caters to the type of player that wants their adventure to feel like a real adventure, navigating by landmarks and a map, camping in the woods, carrying food and rations. It's a game where the journey is essential to the destination, and immersion in the world is one of its biggest features. Actually planning out your trip to the dungeon, clearing the dungeon, and making your way back to town feels like a real journey with logistics and supplies that need to be accounted for.

The world is wide open and fun to explore, with quite a few different and unique environments. These environments even affect what gear you'll be bringing with you - going out into the desert means you'll want to be packing plenty of water and clothing suitable for hot weather. The world feels dangerous and untamed, and even seemingly modest enemies can present a problem for you if you aren't prepared, especially early on. There is quite a bit lore to unpack, and multiple factions to align with for different endings that each play out different stories.

Combat and magic systems are unique and interesting, allowing for a variety of play styles. It's a more methodical and deliberate fighting system, and preparation matters when going into encounters - things like food buffs can make or break whether you survive a fight or not. I really enjoyed messing around with the chakrams the most. It can sometimes feel a little clunky, but there's enough to enjoy here that if you're willing to overlook just a little clunk there is a lot of fun to be had.

In short, if you like the idea of a survival RPG with a methodical, and deliberate fighting system that heavily rewards preparation and strategy, and has very little handholding, but has an interesting and immersive world that is tons of fun to explore and lots of cool lore to dig into, then this is your game.

Me encanta este juego, pero entiendo por que hay gente a quien pueda no gustarle y lo encuentre tedioso. No hay forma rápida de hacer las cosas, pero ese es su encanto. El camino es parte de la aventura, y cosas inesperadas pueden pasar en tu trayectoria que pueden conducirte a un nuevo reto.

Recomiendo este juego, pero solo si eres paciente, y preferiblemente con un amigo.
Difficult, frustrating, annoying but cool.

Outward, released in 2019, feels like a game from early 2000's. I'm not an old school games fan.

And the guy who designed how to change the fps limit option is a psycho.

This game is my escape world. After hours of work, I just enjoy a supposedly an hour play looting every ornate chest in Chersonese before I go to Emmerkar Forest and do the same. Until I realized 4 hours already past meddling to random encounters, going back and forth to sell loot, picking up Gaberries and making Tartines, mining ores until my pickaxe broke, fishing until my harpoon broke, picking up Blue sand at midnight, enjoy strolling in Conflux mountain with purple grass, swinging my Manticore Greatmace and see Bandits fly after hit by Juggernaut, making Horror Weapons and selling them to Levant merchants, listening to relaxing background music, then I go to Harmattan to farm some elemental particles..There goes my supposedly 1-hr play, finished about 6 hrs.. until I feel sleepy. :-D

This game is my total immersion for me.

I'll come back to write this but so far it's a no

Alright I'm back. This game is not worth playing.
You are a weak frail being incapable of fighting 60% of all enemies without 10 buffs and maxed out armor and weapons. Your to slow in combat, your stamina and health is pitiful, and farming for resources just to take 10 potions and 4 magic spells before fighting bigger enemies is not worth your time. If you weren't so weak at every point of this game after the starting map, it'd be a good time. First game where running past every single enemy is a more valid strategy than actually facing most creatures.

If you want a score it gets a 3/10. "Mmmmm yes, 5 death claw dinos kill you just to spawn right back in front of them, mmmmm feels so good...."

The Eraserhead baby of Dark Souls and Breath of the Wild, lacking the core of what made each of those games fun. You run across giant, badly textured Unity maps at half the pace of a Dark Souls character playing 1/4 of an RPG stretched out to an entire RPG's runtime. Minimal character progression means entire character identities are just items. Basically no traversal options means going anywhere is a slog. Ubisoft node based stock gathering and crafting system slapped on the top and ship it out the door. The story is rushed and poorly delivered, basically nothing but fetch quests and the gamefeel of an MMO designed by communists. Magic is needlessly convoluted and basically inaccessible in the fun fantasy adventure title. No pause option and no load option, even in single player. You're not even permitted to take a piss while playing, unless you want to do it in one of the multiudes of times you'll be standing around waiting for gameplay elements to happen in the gameplay loop of this title.

Ultimately, it's just not fun. Everything is a chore. You'll spend 30 minutes delving a dungeon and get a sword that has purple particle effects and takes 17 hits to kill a standard enemy instead of 18 and a single gem. Doing basically anything requires prior knowledge that it's basically impossible to have so you're set up to fail over and over unless you spend your entire life cross referencing a wiki. There are about a dozen adventure games I would recommend someone play before this one that don't punish you for just wanting to play and have much richer environements to explore. You're actually permitted to see while you're playing in most of them as well which is a nice novelty when you've got a tension headache from trying to squint into the bad artwork and poor comprehension of volumetric lighting that the delusional seem to think was an intentional design choice. There is better signposting and lighting in Duke Nukem, a game that has no way to light up it's 2d environments and had no other games to reference.

An artistically bankrupt frankenstein's monster of better games, ♥♥♥♥ out in the cheapest engine available. There is not a single thing in this title that hasn't been done much better elsewhere, and the parts chosen to cobble together the mess weren't even the good parts from the other games. I really just wish that the game didn't take so much of it's runtime getting to the point of the gameplay that I can no longer refund it, because even at 7 dollars I feel like I was ripped off. If, like me, you think; "Oh this is okay it's going to open up to some interesting systems and..." no. Just no. What you experience in that first hour is what you experience again and again and again... and again... And again. With very little variation. You'll get an active skill or two and the number will be bigger. The combat is so bad that you get stat checked out of encounters anyway so it doesn't really matter what skills you choose unless you have the item that you need to fight this enemy, located 2 maps away in an unmarked dungeon. It's so lazy there is just a self contained tutorial on the main menu like this is a platformer from the 1990s because designing a ramp up in your adventure title is too hard. It just starts at 1 pace and remains that that pace for the entire runtime, while talking heads tell you boring ♥♥♥♥ about the politics in a region you don't care about because the game made no attempt to endear you to the place through quests and character interactions, kind of like an adventure game. It is no exaggeration that some of the first adventure games ever made are far superior to this. Ultima Underworld and Daggerfall are much better games. Unless you have a friend and a lot of spare time, play anything else. Play Dora the Explorer because at least there is a ramp up in mechanics and actual progression and not just a diarrhea of slop scattered across the worst map you've ever seen, with random objects just pasted on to the scenery with no thought or reason to them being there. No it's fine it makes perfect sense there is a bandit castle 500m from my tribal village with more living people in it than my entire town. Everyone inside the town lives perfectly normal and happy lives because there is a loading screen between them and the bandits so there is no threat. A fence on the side of the road in the middle of nowhere? Thats fine. That's what fences are for. Being next to roads. Just piles of white rock that don't match the rock in the region and don't even begin to make up the ruins of a structure? That's fine I'm sure they mined the rock 2 maps away where your friend specifically said it an extremely dangerous journey (even while not carrying a rock the size of a person) and then carried those rocks all the way here, and then the starving peasants just repurposed those rocks to make strucutres that aren't in the game. I'm sure it just the ruins of the recent calamity everyone keeps mentioning. The monsters carried off the rocks in this disperate, poorly designed scenery. How did I not think of that sooner? They weren't there to kill and corrupt everyone they were there to take exactly 7/8ths of every single structure. They're very fond of floorboards and mortar you see.

This title reminds me a lot of something that someone I know did where they asked an AI to make Elden Ring with whatever it could find on the internet and then took what it ♥♥♥♥ out and adapted it to actually work. But this is worse. In hindsight all I needed to do was get to the character creator and I knew the exact amount of effort the developer was willing to put in to their players having fun, or play the first 15 minutes of the game to notice how desperately they wanted me to have to suffer their homebrew dungeons and dragons campaign that they can't find a single soul that wants to actually play at the table. Utterly embarassing.

on sale for 5 bucks, you cant beat it. it feels like a single player or 2 player mini version of WoW. it has some great aspects like the dodge system. 

then there is the BAD, like only 8 quick slots for a game that needs 16. Then, it has superior optimization problems. The game looks and plays great as a walking simulator, however, every time you engage a MOB the game freezes for 3-5 seconds. EVERYTIME. this has not, nor ever will be fixed and makes the game almost unplayable at the end game. 

i do love this game, i just hate the optimization. if you kill the motion blur it helps a little, but not enough for me to recommend. especially because this game should run so smooth on my machine. this is a dev issue that never got fixed. it makes me wonder if outward 2 will even be worth it, as this game stayed in EA forever now they say this is the done deal... i think ill get clair obscura instead next time around.

LEARN TO OPTIMIZE! ESPECIALLY DURING COMBAT!!!! jesus.

Incredibly Janky, enemies have attacks that ignore blocking, player attacks feel like they do nothing, inventory is always full making it nearly impossible to move despite being told to come prepared, Open world is a drag to go through because you either never have what you need or have what you need but have to walk in slow-motion. Respawning is horrible because if you're trying to work at a dungeon and trying to figure it out your own way the game punishes you by randomly respawning you in places sometimes the whole map away, which if it were some kind of mercy system it wouldn't be so bad if there was an opt-in, but there isn't so you just get dragged off. The map is awful and does a horrible job of telling you anything, alongside none of the NPC telling you anything, so you can walk up to a place that seems friendly only to find out it's a bandit camp because instead of calling it a Bandit Camp they gave it a fancy name for no reason, meanwhile there are places literally called Bandit Camps. Enemies often spawn in groups, and that wouldn't be so bad if you could lure them away from eachother but they generally move the same speed and stay right next to eachother. Enemies can sometimes simply regen all their HP while you are fighting them if you don't do high enough damage fast enough, often encouraging just running through things, defeating the point of the combat system generally. The survival mechanics are almost entirely meaningless half the time, wherein you will always have so much food that it is pointless, or have so little food that you won't even be engaging with the game anymore until you get food again, water is so easily gotten and filtered that there is almost no point to it being a required resource. Enemies constantly apply huge debuffs even from the beginning whereas your strongest attack is Kick... Friendly NPCs will often just stand by and watch you die in front of them, even when they are actively telling you they want to fight and/or escape. The game also explains not a whole lot, which would be fine if it weren't so awkward, like playing a game not made by someone who played a video game before, but was just explained it, or just watched it. There is very little respect for player expression or time, so ultimately I have to give this a Negative review unless I find something in it worth doing, but for the moment I am dropping it because I am tired of being warped away from a dungeon I am practicing fighting enemies in back to Cierzo for the 14th time.

10/10 | A Brutal, Beautiful Adventure That Refuses to Hold Your Hand

Outward is one of the few RPGs that made me feel like a real adventurer instead of a chosen one wrapped in plot armor.

Most games tell you you're a hero. Outward hands you a backpack, points vaguely toward the horizon, and says, "Good luck. Try not to freeze to death."

Every journey feels earned. Every victory feels personal. You don't win because you're the Dragonborn, the Savior, or some ancient prophecy. You win because you prepared. You packed enough food. You remembered your bandages. You paid attention to the weather. And when you finally survive a fight that nearly killed you three times, it feels incredible.

The world is unforgiving, but that's exactly what makes it memorable. Getting lost in the wilderness, stumbling into enemies far stronger than you, desperately trying to make it back to town with barely any health left... those moments create stories that scripted quests never could.

The co-op experience is also something special. Few games capture the feeling of two exhausted adventurers limping back to camp after a disaster, laughing at how badly everything went wrong. Some of my favorite gaming memories came from Outward's chaos.

What makes the game truly stand out is that it respects the player. It doesn't flood your map with markers or constantly tell you where to go. Exploration feels genuine. Discovery feels rewarding. Success feels deserved.

It's rough around the edges sometimes, but those rough edges are part of its identity. Beneath them is one of the most unique RPG experiences ever made.

10/10. Not because it's perfect, but because no other game delivers the same feeling of adventure.

And now, I'm counting the days until Outward 2 arrives. Everything I've seen suggests it's keeping the soul of the original while improving combat, immersion, world design, and character progression and I genuinely haven't been this excited for an RPG in years.

If Outward was a weathered adventurer's journal filled with scars, bad decisions, and unforgettable stories, Outward 2 looks ready to become the next chapter.

Backpack packed. Waterskin filled.

I'm ready to get absolutely destroyed by Aurai all over again. ⚔️🔥🌲

IMPORTANT: Outward has severe performance issues on certain hardware configurations in two of its major cities: Levant and Harmattan. I have an Nvidia Geforce RTX 3070 with 8GB of VRAM, which is above the recommended settings, yet entering these cities causes my computer fan to spin up like a jet engine. Reducing the graphics settings to minimum did not help, only cutting the FPS from 60 to 30 while inside the cities did.

The developers have moved on to work on Outward 2, so this will likely never be fixed.

If you have a similar hardware configuration to mine, think carefully about buying this. It's possible to work around this, as I mentioned, but you might not find it worth the tedium.

---

Re-writing this review after playing another 20 more hours. I stand by my initial statement: Outward is an excellent if niche game, but the performance issues are a sore point.

That said, the fact that I decided to push through and work around the performance issues is a testament to its appeal.

Outward is designed for the specific niche of 'Action RPG + harsh survival mechanics'. If you played Fallout 4's survival mode, or Kingdom Come Deliverance 1 or 2's hardcore modes, and enjoyed the survival mechanics but wished things were even harsher- this is for you. Another game with similar appeal would be The Long Dark.

Some reviews may mention Dark Souls, but I reckon most Dark Souls fans would not enjoy this game. The combat mechanics are not particularly satisfying. Death has minimal cost in Dark Souls, enticing players to try again and master the game mechanics, or experiment with different approaches. Death in Outward usually has harsh penalties, and *autosaves on death* (with no manual save), so it encourages playing things cautiously and running away as soon as things start to go badly.

The average action RPG rewards player mastery of combat mechanics (Inputs, dodging, etc). Whereas in Outward, gameplay is about planning your route, preparing the right buffs, and luring enemies to fight each other. 

In other RPGs, stacking buffs and resistances until you reach 100% immunity to some types of damage is exploiting the game mechanics. In Outward, the entire game is balanced around you stacking buffs and resistances, and higher level enemies will murder you in a few seconds if you don't.

In other RPGs, abusing enemy AI to lure them onto 10 traps/land mines in a row is slightly cheesy. In Outward, there is an entire skill tree themed around luring enemies into traps. And so on.

Outward's philosophy is: You're not the hero, you're just a weak generic villager, and the world is balanced against you. Here's a bag of tools to level the playing field, or tilt it in your favour. The game's difficulty is balanced around the player deploying every advantage they have, from traps to buffs to abusing enemy AI, so there's no need to hold back or 'play fair'.

If this sounds good to you, consider buying Outward (performance issues notwithstanding). If this sounds terrible to you, I would advise against getting it. Even as part of Outward's target audience, I found the game quite exasperating at times. If you're not part of the aforementioned audience, you'll probably hate it.
If it needs mods to feel like a decent game, I can't recommend it.

Outward is a game of all time. One might say it is a survival game, others might say it is a role playing game. Outward is neither of these things. Outward, in and of itself, is a test of character. It is a game where you will be fighting yourself more than the things within the game. It will teach you consistency and preparedness. It will teach you discipline. Depending on what kind of player you are, you may succumb to cheese, or surmount the challenge of Aurai itself.

This game is a transformative journey.

I really enjoyed the feeling and vibe of the adventure in this game. The world is well built and the graphic is surprisingly good and colorful! Everything is very saturated however. The combat is unfortunately clunky and not good. arm swinging feels clunky and combat is actually more difficult than darksouls series as it is so awkward. Its punishing and if you die, you go to this jail and have to escape yourself. That is on top of losing all your stuff.

Before battle, you have to remove your backpack so you can move faster and more stamina and it made me feel like I am fist fighting in grade 3 after school. It is definitely more fun to play with another friend as solo is extremely hard. I would recommend this game if the combat was not so clunky and punishing- It is such a needy game as you need to control your hunger by feeding and thirst, your temperature to have certain equipments etc. Feels like i would have fun if i was a veteran of the game with more experience but the clunky battle wont even let me try the game further.

It was fun to play with you my best friend BK - Rest in peace

Quest rewards are only given to the host, including but not limited to:
Items
Skills
Recipes

So quest-only skills can only be obtained by either playing game on 2 different saves (switching host and following the same questline) or basically hacking using developer tools to grant missed stuff to the guest. I got it for $2 per, and the rule of "an hour of fun for a dollar" works for that, but if you're buying it for any more than $3 I wouldn't do it.

I almost passed this game up after reading some of the negative reviews...I'm so glad I gave it a chance, my 9 year old son and I have played for dozens of hours together in coop and yes the game can be difficult at times but a change of tactics or better preparation allows you to go right back in and prevail. After over 100 hours in the game now myself, I can say its one of my favorite games of all time. Do not pass it up if you like a challenge.

This game is really good. Its problem is that it doesn't do a good job of explaining the hell you're about to jump into. This is a very niche, (in the beginning) very difficult and demanding game. It will not tell you what to do 90% of the time. It will not show you where to go most of the time. You will get molly-whopped in combat at various stages of the game - if you've played and enjoyed games like DS or Elden Ring, you might have more luck here. I've played them all and still got my ass handed to me. 

Above all, it's not for everyone. But when you market yourself to everyone, you're inevitably going to end up with people who aren't a good fit and that's how you speedrun getting mixed/bad review scores. 

If you don't know which camp you're in, I would say give it a try. You'll definitely know if it is or isn't for you within the 2-hour Steam refund window. If you look past some of the faults it definitely has (too much walking without a ton to see, for example), it's a surprisingly fun game with a lot to do AFTER the main story concludes. I think most people try to explore right from the get-go when they're at their most vulnerable and un-surprisingly get rolled by something they're not prepared to face. Gear is everything in Outward. Skill and abilities definitely make a difference, don't get me wrong. But no matter how good you are, getting one-shot by something is always frustrating. And without gear, that's what you can expect.

I should add, once you're geared for the area your current main quest is in, exploration and survival is much more manageable. There are some really tough places in the first area that will still be difficult after finishing the main quest-line, depending on, of course, your gear.

Good game. there were a few areas of the game I would have preferred structured differently.  I died a lot.  But it is a classic somewhat.
Fun.

I love this game. Its not for everyone, but not every game should be. It is a fantasy adventure with survival elements that make you feel more immersed in the world, because of this a lot of people with find the game needlessly slow or hard to understand. The game gives you everything you need to survive, but you have to find out what you need to thrive. I've seen the combat compared to dark souls and that is a huge mischaracterization of the game. while the combat is deliberate and punishing, the stamina system is much different than any of the souls games making each encounter feel deliberate, you aren't able to chain one encounter to the next and have to weigh your resources before each battle. It is 100% a walking simulator for quite a bit of the game, but because of this aspect i have had literal jumpscares by hearing a noise of a dangerous enemy walking by that i would just ignore with any fast travel system.

This game isnt for everyone, but if you are willing to be hungry, tired, thirsty, diseased, and hexed, then this game will be quite the adventure for the travelers brave enough to venture into Aurai.

The story is nothing to spectacular, but this game is truly about the journey you go through and if you manage to beat a hardcore with no deaths, you will truly have a story to tell.
